coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanoparticles are well-characterized as nanotubes, nano diamonds, graphene and carbon dots. Their unique properties present promising applications in nanomedicine, including drug delivery systems. However, the cell-damaging effect of carbon-based nanoparticles remains elusive. Studies on carbon-caused cell deaths are contradictory, which makes it challenging to claim their precise nature, mechanisms, and harmful dosage. Moreover, previous findings showed that immune cells are the most susceptible cells to carbon nanoparticle treatment, where cell viability differs depending on cell culture and treatment specificities. Considering the shortage of topic-specific summarized data and rising interest in carbon nanomaterials, the present review article focuses on the cytotoxicity of carbon, in terms of cell viability, and types of cell deaths induced by carbon nanoparticles.

Signaling cascade induced by carbon nanoparticles of graphene, nanodiamond, nanotubes.
Carbon nanoparticles (and carbon dots) induced apoptosis signaling. High doses carbon nanoparticles induce PARP cleavage and low doses carbon nanoparticles induce drug resistance [69–72,76,77].

Oxidative stress, cytotoxicity increases with the increase in diameter and rigidness, length and concentration

Least toxic, but in certain doses may cause oxidative damage to the DNA and instability of chromatins, causes osteogenesis without cytotoxicity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanoparticles are well-characterized as nanotubes, nano diamonds, graphene and carbon dots. Their unique properties present promising applications in nanomedicine, including drug delivery systems. However, the cell-damaging effect of carbon-based nanoparticles remains elusive. Studies on carbon-caused cell deaths are contradictory, which makes it challenging to claim their precise nature, mechanisms, and harmful dosage. Moreover, previous findings showed that immune cells are the most susceptible cells to carbon nanoparticle treatment, where cell viability differs depending on cell culture and treatment specificities. Considering the shortage of topic-specific summarized data and rising interest in carbon nanomaterials, the present review article focuses on the cytotoxicity of carbon, in terms of cell viability, and types of cell deaths induced by carbon nanoparticles.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Small scales such as nanoscales allow to minimize the toxicity of drugs, ensure delivery to target cells, and perform quantum behavior to improve imaging of specific compounds within the body. These abilities can be achieved by controlling and managing the nanoparticles’ shape, size, structure, and composition. Compared to other elements, carbon is the most suitable element for nano-constructions [1]. Carbon nanoparticles (further CNPs) include a variety of carbon-based structures, namely carbon dots, and
s
p
2
hybridized carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes, fullerene, graphene, and nanodiamonds [1]. Although many CNPs, including nanotubes, nanodiamonds and graphene, are composed of only carbon, they form different chemical structures, bonding types, and atomic arrangements. Therefore, these CNPs have good heat and electrical conductivity, high strength and physical stability, and low cytotoxicity due to high biocompatibility, and, for
s
p
2
CNPs, disability to dissolve in water [1]. Besides, the combination of such properties as modification of the surface, stabilization of loaded drugs, and adaptability of size makes CNPs a beneficial target for drug delivery and nanomedicine investigations. For instance, carbon dots were reported to be modifiable through numerous techniques, including polymer capping, doping and coating with specific compounds, allowing their use for various biomedical purposes [2]. One study investigated the relationship between L-amino acids and amyloid activity based on carbon nanoparticles with amino acids-modified surfaces [3], while mesoporous carbon nanoparticles presented large surface areas, tunable size, and pH-dependent release of drugs [4]. Furthermore, considering these advantageous properties, CNPs have a high potential to be used widely in many fields, including the food industry due to their antimicrobial properties [5], electronics such as batteries [6], agriculture [7], and medicine [8,9,10]. For example, according to recent research, carbon nanoparticles could be utilized to design energy storage devices, ensuring high energy conductivity capacity, and contributing to environmental sustainability by being produced from organic materials, such as coconut oil [6]. Nowadays, one of the well-studied areas of CNPs is nanomedicine, such as regenerative medicine, drug delivery, diagnostics, bioimaging, etc. (Fig. 1 ) [11] . Some studies suggest the applications of CNPs for the delivery of chemical drugs, such as target-specific delivery systems for cytotoxic agents. In contrast, some studies were focused on the CNPs’ cytotoxicity itself, since they allow an understanding of under which circumstances the risk of cell damage is higher, whether it depends on the dose of nanoparticles or not, and how different types of CNPs determine the extent of toxic effects. These research points might be beneficial for constructing a broader picture of using CNPs in nanomedicine (Table 1 ).

In this review, we aimed to gather updated data about different types of CNPs and their effect on cell viability to consolidate the existing information about the toxicity of CNPs into one source. Previous investigations studying different cell types exposed to several types of carbon nanoparticles were searched. Most of the described papers were obtained from the PubMed source. Some other databases were the ACS website, Google Scholar and Google. Most related articles were searched by keywords such as carbon nanoparticles, cell apoptosis, cytotoxicity, nanodiamond, nanotubes, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanoparticle types and their characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: Several most prominent and frequently studied types of CNPs and their general physical, chemical, and biocompatible properties are widely studied (Fig. 1). Together with properties and potential application, the effect of each of them on different types of cells, including, but not limited to, epithelial, cancer and immune cells are also considered in this review, including the underlying mechanisms triggered by these CNPs.

The hollow, nanometer-scale cylinders, known as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), which have numerous benefits over spherical particles, are emerging as potential drug delivery systems: the open mouths of CNTs allow them to explore the interior surface and incorporate other compounds into the tubes which represents them as a potential of pharmaceuticals delivery [12]. These CNTs may be composed of a single wall (SWCNTs) or multiple walls (MWCNTs). As they are immune to microbial attack and do not bloat or become more porous with the pH changes, the CNTs are capable of efficiently defending against denaturation brought on by deterioration of the external environment molecules that are trapped inside [12].

Carbon-based nanomaterials is promising in the prevention and treatment of cancer cells. Recent research has investigated MWCNTs as nanoplatforms for biomedical purposes [13]. However, the key contentious issue for many articles is whether carbon nanotubes have cytotoxic characteristics. Some studies contend that these elements might also be carcinogenic, though. The results of one study demonstrated that high concentrations of short SWCNTs and MWCNTs cause pulmonary and pleural inflammation in various ways and may spread throughout the entire body following intratracheal instillation [14]. There is evidence that nanoparticles have cytotoxicity towards LA9 fibroblasts, and their presence in the cell will lead to oxidative stress and eventually to apoptosis [15]. Carbon nanomaterials’ cytotoxicity has primarily been classified according to their type (SWCNTs, MWCNTs, carbon nanohorns), form, rigidity, length, and lastly dependent on their concentration.

SWCNTs caused autophagic cell death in the highly resistant cancer cell line A549 from human lung carcinoma, as demonstrated by Liu et al. [16]. In 2020, Fraser et al. illustrated that increasing length and diameter lead to increased epithelial cell toxicity: the research has demonstrated that the proportion of fibers with longer nominal lengths and diameters affects the higher toxicity of MWCNTs toward human lung epithelial cells (BEAS-2B). A similar pattern in which greater cytotoxicity was associated with hard and needle-shaped MWCNT with a diameter of >50 µm was noticed in other studies too [17]. In comparison, the findings of Zhao et al. [13] imply that with an increase in MWCNT diameter the toxicity of MWCNTs could be reduced based on the same mass concentration.

However, most studies suggest that the increased size and length of carbon nanotubes could lead to higher cytotoxicity. Xu et al. [18] found that large MWCNT had a higher probability of resulting in asbestos-like pleural lesions that are important for the development of mesothelioma: when large MWCNT was administered to the lung, it was discovered in the pleural cavity, accumulated in the parietal pleura, and increased inflammatory responses there. With longer MWCNT exposure being linked to more morphological alterations in human vein endothelial cells and relatively higher cytotoxicity to human vein endothelial cells; these findings are consistent with some earlier research utilizing several cell lines that demonstrated longer MWCNTs were more cytotoxic than shorter ones [19]. Macrophages subjected to higher quantities of the long, rigid MWCNTs showed signs of autophagic cell death, additionally, macrophages exposed to the latter MWCNTs showed lysosomal destruction along with the cytosolic release of cathepsin B [20]. The toxic patterns could be explained by metallic impurities contained in longer MWCNTs. During the synthesis of CNT metals such as iron, cobalt and nickel which are used as catalysts. These metal residues may remain inside of the tubes due to the protection by a graphitic shell. Studies showed that metal particles after the leakage out of the nanotubes have even higher free radical-generated cytotoxicity compared to the cytotoxicity of the nanotube itself [21].

Interestingly, one study showed that for both long and short-term CNT exposure strategies, higher amounts of DNA damage were seen across dosages and time points [22]. The maximum dose of long CNTs administered intravenously caused fibrosis, but lower lung-deposited doses received through inhalation did not cause such lung disease [22]. CNTs’ concentration is essential for cytotoxicity. One data showed that at high concentrations, these nanomaterials might deregulate cell cycle and induce apoptosis, and the underlying pathways have been studied [23,20].

Another field where carbon-based nanomaterials have increasing usages is agriculture. In farming conditions, CNTs are gaining popularity as alternative fertilizers that could increase output levels while minimizing adverse environmental effects [24]. For example, CNTs showed the ability to intensify the provision of water and supplements necessary for growth, including nitrogen, potassium, and phosphorus, by reducing the number of hydrogen ions [24]. Overall, CNTs present great potential to be studied further due to their different effect on plant cells, including the influence on the germination of tomato and mustard seeds by providing nutrients [25,26].

Nanodiamonds are small diamonds of size less than 100 nm. Nanodiamonds have the crystal backbone of a diamond cubic structure, due to which nanodiamonds have most of the basic chemical properties of bulk diamonds, such as stability and low water solubility.

Nowadays, nanodiamonds have been widely studied, owing to their convenient properties such as chemical inertness and stability, and the ability to be doped with other molecules [27], which is very useful in drug delivery. Due to their high wear resistance, nanodiamonds in implants prevent the leak of metallic ions from the implant to internal organs [28]. For the same reason, nanodiamonds are actively used for regenerative biomedical applications. In addition, nanodiamonds are used in the modification of medical biomaterials for the creation of surfaces, which will not interfere with the structure of other materials. In comparison with other carbon particles carbon nanodiamonds were found to be the most biocompatible ones [29]. However, studies focused on the cytotoxicity of nanodiamond particles (nDPs) have shown that at the concentration of 50 µg/mL, nDPs showed cytotoxicity and lead to apoptosis by inhibiting cell proliferation. Moreover, oxidative damage to the DNA and instability of chromatins after treatment with nanodiamonds was observed. Cells exposed to nDPs were especially vulnerable against single-strand DNA break, while double-strand DNA break was not shown, still nucleus organisation was highly affected by nanodiamond treatment [30]. For instance, at a concentration of 10 µg/mL, nDPs could induce the production of micronuclei [30]. Thus, it was concluded that carbon nanodiamond particles’ biocompatibility is limited by their cause to damage DNA [30]. On the other hand, in vivo experiments showed different results: mice subcutaneously injected with nDPs had no inflammation, while mice exposed to intratracheal instillation with nDPs were affected by acute toxicity [30,31]. Through this experiment, it was proven that nDPs can pass the air-blood barrier from the lungs to blood circulation. The highest accumulation of nanodiamond particles was in the lungs and due to comparatively easier penetration of particles to blood, the second highest accumulation was in the spleen and liver [31]. The retention time of nDPs in the lungs was 48 h and such a long duration caused significant toxicity [31].

Investigations have found that depending on a cell type, nanodiamonds can cause different effects and the most susceptible one among experimented cells was lymphocytes. Neuroblastoma cells treated with 2–10 nm sized nanodiamonds were not affected, while macrophages treated with the same size nDPs for 24 h had significantly lower viability at the nDP concentration range of 50–100 µg/mL [32]. In contrast, human endothelial cells had decreased viability [33] at longer exposure (72-h long treatment) with even smaller nDPs of size 2–5 nm. In endothelial cells, nDPs were found to induce apoptosis, as the production of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species was observed [34]. Similar results were obtained in experiments performed with lymphocytes [30]. However, in macrophages and neuroblastoma cells production of reactive species was not observed. Some experiments with high concentrations were shown that at short treatment periods, nanodiamonds are not toxic. Toxic effects of 100 nm sized nDPs at 400 µg/mL concentration were not observed after treating kidney 293T cells for 3 h [35]. During another investigation, monoblast-like U937 cells were also found to be sensitive to nanodiamond treatment, as their viability significantly decreased at 0.2 mg/mL, while SaOS-2 cells (osteoblasts) had increased osteogenesis without cytotoxicity [36].

Graphene is a single-layer thick lattice of
s
p
2
carbon atoms. Lately, graphene has become prominent due to wide usages such as drug delivery, bioimaging [37], biosensors [38], and biomaterials. Previously, it was proven that some modified graphene is not toxic, for example, in vivo experiments where mice were injected with polyethylene glycol-functionalized (PEGylated) graphene did not show an accumulation of nanoparticles inside the body for a long time and these NPs were eventually metabolised out of the organism without causing toxicity [39]. In vitro experiments with graphene oxide did not cause cell death, even though oxidative stress was observed [40]. However, experiments with pristine graphene showed that unmodified graphene can be cytotoxic and can lead to apoptotic cell death by several pathways. Macrophages treated with graphene had increased levels of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), which further triggered the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signalling cascade and mitochondrial apoptotic pathway. Extracellular trigger of apoptosis was also found to be involved, the transforming growth factor-beta (TGFβ) signalling pathway was activated after treatment with graphene, leading to the activation of pro-apoptotic Bim and Bax proteins from the B-cell lymphoma-2 (Bcl-2) family [41]. During another study, it was found that unmodified graphene induces cytokine and chemokine production of macrophages through activation of the nuclear factor-kappa B (NF-ĸB) pathway (Fig. 2 ). Cytokines and chemokines released by graphene-affected macrophages have negative immune responses, as released factors decrease the ability for extracellular matrix adhesion and phagocytic activity of naive macrophages [42].

Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the smallest CNPs, as their size is less than 10 nm. Similar to previously described CNPs, CDs also have important characteristics, such as low toxicity and biocompatibility, making CDs suitable for biomedical applications. However, compared to other CNPs, CDs have a photoluminescence property and its fluorescence is the most prominent characteristic of CDs, which increases its usage potential in bioimaging. Another distinguishing feature of CDs is its small size and availability of the surface for doping with other elements or organic functional groups, making it convenient for tissue-specific drug delivery [43]. They are also utilized in materials, synthetic chemistry, biosensing and disease diagnosis [44]. Moreover, CDs were found to contain carbon nanoparticles that prevent corrosion. Effective tweaking of CDs' intrinsic structure and surface state is made possible through functionalization [45]. CDs can be synthesized from organic materials, such as garlic, milk, orange juice, lemon peel, beet and soybean by hydrothermal method [46,47]. Therefore, the synthesis of CDs can be considered environmentally friendly, as they were produced from less toxic materials and do not consist of hazardous chemicals.

From a quantum mechanics perspective, it was identified that carbon nanoparticles strongly interact with the backbone of DNA phosphate, which further inhibits the DNA repair system promoted by dephosphorylation [47]. One of the already existing therapies against cancer has a similar mechanism, an inhibitor of poly ADP-ribose polymerase (PARP), a family of proteins responsible for single-strand DNA repair and maintaining genomic stability. Together with PARP inhibitor, carbon nanoparticles were examined for their effects on cell viability. It was shown that at low doses carbon dots will promote resistance against PARP inhibitors, while at high doses carbon dots will promote cell death through AQP protein and extracellular matrix, and DNA damage (Fig. 3 ) [47]. Such a dose-dependent effect can be explained by the phosphatase-like nanozyme activity [48] of carbon nanoparticles and their associated siganling pathways. To induce “synthetic lethality” of cancer cells, the combination of carbon nanoparticles together with inhibitors of phosphatase and PARP was proposed by investigators [47].

A similar trend was observed in the effect of CNPs on plants. The optimal concentration of CNPs was found to be crucial for its successful agricultural application. CNPs could interfere with plant growth if their high amount is accumulated on the roots leading to damage by ROS [49]. Another essential function of carbon-based nanoparticles is the delivery of chemicals to plants. There is evidence that carbon nanofibers (CNFs) efficiently brought copper micronutrients to plants contributing to increased germination and growth [50].

Cell death is the process when cell functioning is terminated. The most common types are apoptosis, the programmed energy-dependent cell death regulated by the activity of caspases, and necrosis, rapid non-programmed cell death mediated by calpains and lysosomal proteases [51,52]. Apoptosis can be caused by DNA damage, insufficiency of survival factors, activation of death receptors, oxidative stress, or dysregulated metabolism, while stronger triggers, such as trauma and ischemia lead to necrosis [52]. One of the subtypes of necrosis is necroptosis which could be stimulated by tumor necrosis factor (TNF) and observed in patients with ischemia-reperfusion injury (IRI) of the kidney or brain [53]. Another type of cell death is ferroptosis, an iron-dependent process which involves ROS accumulation and leads to unfavourable conditions for cell survival [53]. Pyroptosis is immunogenic cell death which is caused by pathogens, characterized as a lytic process, and followed by inflammatory pathways [54]. For autophagy, during this process, the cell degrades its organelles inside of the double membrane structure called autophagosome. In general, autophagosome is considered a survival mechanism, but some elements of the autophagic pathway may lead to cell death [55].

Apoptosis is highly regulated by caspases, which are activated both intrinsically and extrinsically. Extrinsic pathway includes the activation of death receptor proteins on the membrane by signaling molecules. Whereas intrinsic events include changes inside the cell, like low oxygen availability, oxidative stress, DNA damage, and permeabilization of the mitochondrial membrane. The most known pro-apoptotic agents induce apoptosis by ROS production or by DNA damage. The induction of apoptosis by CNPs differs based on the shape and dose of CNPs. It was shown that graphene and SWCNT are more cytotoxic compared to the 3-dimensional nanostructure made up of both of them (G/SWCNT) [56]. These G/SWCNT nanoparticles induce mitochondrial apoptosis in osteosarcoma cells via ROS production. However, in vivo, the injection of G/SWCNT into mice didn't cause any damage to internal organs or inflammation. Similar effects of graphene on cells were also observed: after the exposure to graphene the outer membrane of mitochondria was permeabilized, leading to the change of membrane potential in mitochondria. Graphene also increased ROS production, affecting TGF-β and MAPK signalling pathways and further activating Caspase 3 proteins [41]. However, depending on the presence of functional groups graphene may act in different ways: graphene oxide leads to ROS-mediated apoptosis, while graphene oxide-COOH activates ROS-independent apoptosis [57].

Autophagy is a protective process targeting cell survival during a lack of nutrients and energy deficiency. There are 2 types of autophagy: micro- and macroautophagy. During macroautophagy, organelles are degraded by phagophore, which matures into autophagosomes and fuses with endosomes and lysosomes [58]. In contrast to it, microautophagy is the process when cytoplasmic compartments are engulfed by septation and protrusion of the lysosome membrane. They also differ in selectivity, as macroautophagy can be either selective or non-selective as well, while microautophagy is considered to be selective. Macroautophagy's selectivity is based on the interaction of autophagosomes with Atg8 protein [58]. Regulators of autophagy also include the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) protein kinase, AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), and phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PtdIns3K) [58]. Recently, it was found that several nanoparticles including carbon nanotubes and fullerenes can induce autophagic cell death through inhibition of mTOR or through inducing phosphorylation of BCL2 and autophagy-related proteins [59] (Fig. 4 ). Studies showed that single and multi-walled carbon nanotubes [60], and graphene oxide [61] CNPs induce the accumulation of autophagosomes, and in particular graphene oxide can induce autophagosome activation through the toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling pathway [62]. Fullerene may lead to the blockage of autophagy and lysosomal trafficking disruption. However, the effect of fullerene depends on the concentration, because it was shown that at cytotoxic concentration fullerene can cause autophagy and even advance toxicity of chemotherapeutics. Therefore, depending on the concentration, carbon nanoparticles can either disrupt or induce autophagy [63]. Other factors affecting the toxicity of carbon nanoparticles include the presence and type of functional groups on the surface of CNPs. For example, it was shown that carbon nanotubes with the carboxyl (COOH) group induce autophagy and cellular death in adenocarcinoma cells via the decreased rate of mTOR phosphorylation, while polyethylene glycol tagged CNTs did not show these effects [16].

Necrosis is a non-specific type of death resulting mostly from physical trauma. Therefore, it has been considered accidental cell death not caused by a specific signaling pathway. Necrosis, except for trauma, could be induced by toxin exposure, or bacterial and viral infection. Initially, there is a phase of recovery that cells can undergo after passing a certain threshold. This threshold is highly connected to the separation of mitochondrial membranes, which leads to the irreparable loss of ability for oxidative phosphorylation. The last step includes general disorganization of other organelles and subcellular compartments [58]. Similar to autophagy, in the case of necrosis cell viability and cell death type highly depend on the concentration of CNPs as well. For example, it was shown that fullerene at high doses induces necrosis through ROS production, while low doses of CNPs resulted in autophagy without ROS mediation in glioma cells [64].

Ferroptosis is an iron and ROS-dependent type of cell death, which is not similar to previously mentioned cell death types. Initially, it was found out that the oncogenic agent erastin induces cell death which is not like any other known type of cellular death and was named ferroptosis [65]. Mesoporous carbon nanoparticles loaded with iron carbonyl (FeCO) and doxorubicin (DOX) were tested for cytotoxicity of breast cancer cells (MCF-7). The results showed a decrease in cell viability via the release of carbon monoxide (CO), which increased the susceptibility of cancer cells to chemotherapeutic agents [66]. The addition of ferroptosis inhibitor, ferrostatin-1, increased cell viability, proving the activation of ferroptotic cell death [66]. Another study showed the correlation of carbon quantum dots (CQD)-based nanozyme's oxidase-like activity with the induction of ferroptosis in cancer cells. Tumor-bearing mice treated with CQD had higher immune response against tumor cells and in general suppressed tumor growth [67].

In summary, different types of CNPs have both similar and different characteristics. Carbon nanotubes present potential benefits for drug delivery and cancer prevention and treatment. This conclusion was based on its structural properties and the ability to resist microbial attack and denaturation caused by pH changes. Carbon nanodiamonds and graphene were found to mostly affect immune cells and, up to recent studies, both are found to be significantly less cytotoxic compared to other types of cells. Graphene induces apoptosis in immune cells by upregulating the NF-ĸB pathway, but the molecular mechanisms of nanodiamond-induced apoptosis remain elusive. Both graphene and nanodiamonds showed different results in monoculture and co-culture of several cell types. It was observed that nanodiamonds destabilize the nuclear organization of exposed cells, while carbon dots in cancer cells together with PARP inhibitors may interfere with the DNA stability due to the intrinsic phosphatase activity of carbon dots. In a similar manner, carbon nanotubes were also found to target DNA of exposed cells. Therefore, further studies on molecular mechanisms of CNP on genome stability are required. Moreover, dates derived carbon dots can inhibit peroxidase [69]. Carbon dots derived from tea can induce p14ARF with dysregulation of YAP; derived from soybean can induce DNA damage when combined with MET kinase inhibitor; derived from ginger can induce p53 and apoptosis thereby inhibiting cancer cell growth [70–72] (Fig. 3). In summary, carbon dots mediated cytotoxicity was early studied in liver cancr cells by 53 pathways [72], but detailed signaling needs to be comprehensive investigated in addition to recent reports on water channel protein AQP, fibrinectin, DNA damage pathways by multi-omics methods besides the proteomics already reported [47] (Fig. 3). Therefore, more studies on molecular mechanisms of CNP induced cell death for further developing in vivo treatment are required.

For research methods mediated differential results, practical applications might be limited to the cost- and time-effectiveness concerns of in vivo studies, thus these points should be considered before investigations (Fig. 5 ). In healthcare, the targeted effect of carbon nanoparticles on immune cells might gain more scientific interest and result in extended knowledge about the immunogenicity of CNPs or the toxicity minimization approaches.

Different types of CNPs exhibit common and various characteristics. Depending on their types, characteristics, and doses, CNPs showed distinct or common effect on cytotoxicity. Carbon nanodiamonds and graphene were found to mostly target immune cells. In general, several studies have concluded that depending on some factors (such as the type, size, and concentration of CNPs, combined treatment with other drugs or monotreatment, the type of the cell and exposure time for in vitro experiments, injection methods for in vivo experiments) results may contradict each other. These contradictions lead to the remaining knowledge gap about how CNPs act in different settings.
